TRANSCRIPT
Operation Quick Start -- this is the first steps, the first steps to get rolling in your
business.
I'm assuming you're going to have an online business and probably an information
business, a "wisdom business", like I like to say. The first step is you have to find
your market, and you might already have done this, but this is a big one. You have to
find your market. Now, within your market ... Well, first of all, there are three big "mega
niches" that I call them. The first one is money; the second one is health; and the third
one is relationships. Each one of these has a thousand sub markets.
So, my current business is teaching people how to build their business, teaching
entrepreneurship, teaching people how to launch, and that's part of the money niche. It's
about building a business to make more money, to make an income. And actually, my last
business, which was teaching people about the stock market ... that was part of the money
niche as well. It could be about saving up for retirement; it could be about getting
promotions at work; it could be about moving to another country and living as an expat in
retirement, and that sort of covers a whole bunch of different things, but a big chunk of
that is money. As you can see, money's got a lot of different sub-niches -- health is the
same way.
It could be about bodybuilding; it could be about beating cancer; it could be about
having more energy in the day. Relationships -- it could be about finding a love
relationship. It could be about parenting; it could be about being a better sibling. It could
be about your relationship with yourself. It could be about relationship with your
spirituality. Those three -- the money, health, and relationships -- they're these huge,
huge, mega niches that ... So, you can't have a business about money -- it can't be just
about money. You have to dial into a sub niche and probably a sub, sub niche. Now, in
addition to those three, there's also hobbies, and sports, special interests -- it could be
Chevy Colorado pickup trucks. And then what you can do is if you're within a hobby, or
sport, or special interest, you can overlay one of those on top of one of the mega niches.
For example, if maybe the hobby that you're interested in, building a business
around, is photography. Well, you could teach people how to take photos, but you could
also overlay it with the money niche and maybe teach people how to make money from
their photography. Or maybe you're teaching tennis, so that's your niche is tennis. Well,

you could overlay that with health and it could be strength training for tennis players. Or
maybe you have a business around travel, and you could overlay that with relationships,
and it could be international travel with small kids, or meeting the love of your life in
Latin America.
So there's lots and lots of different ways this can break out, and this ... finding your
market, finding your niche -- I could go way deeper than I can within Operation Quick
Start here. But it's about finding your place, finding where your interests, and your
hobbies, and your passions can line up with a business. One way to think about this is
what do people come to you for help? What if people approach you ... people that you
know, what do they come to you for? What advice to they come to you for? And that could
be a great start. That could be a great start in finding your niche.
So, again, this isn't something that happens overnight. It's not like, "Boom,
you've got your niche; you got your market," it's something that's gradual. For me,
with that first business, I'd been studying the stock market for years, so it seemed like a
natural. I started publishing about the stock market. Actually, I was publishing for mutual
fund investors -- that was my initial niche. Then, I actually met a partner, someone I
ended partnering with, and he had a lot of knowledge about the day trading market, so I
started with mutual fund investors and then I pivoted and I started serving day traders.
So, sometimes you circle around until you get your actual niche.
Once you start to nail down what your niche or your market is, then
you have to find your people. You find the people you're gonna serve. Understanding
your avatars is absolutely critical because you are probably not your market. You are not
your market -- this is something I repeat over, and over, and over when I'm coaching
people. You are not your market. What I mean by that is when I started publishing about
the stock market, I'd been studying the market obsessively for several years, so I had a
different level of knowledge and experience than the people I was publishing to because
most of the people in most markets are beginners, are newbies -- have far less knowledge
and wisdom than you do.
You always have to remember that you're not your market and
they're much more beginner, much more newbie than you are. You probably
have a much greater insight into what they need, with the knowledge and the skills that
they need. Often what they need isn't what they think they need, and it's probably not
what they want. If you're going to teach men how to get dates, how to pick up women, so
to speak, they probably have a lot of work to do on their inner game on who they are to

become more attractive, but really they think all they need is some pickup lines. So, I have
a phrase and it's, "Sell them what they want, and give them what they need."
You have to sell them the pickup lines knowing that that's not really what's going to
serve them, and then as soon as they get into your world, yeah, you give them the pickup
lines, but you start educating them immediately that, "You know what, I'll give you the
pickup lines and they're gonna help you, but they're not gonna help you nearly as much as
if you become a better human being by doing these things." Sell them what they want,
give them what they need.
Now, when it comes back to finding your people, you have to look at where they are
gathering. Where are they gathering? Now, these days, this is so much easier because of
social media. Different groups can gather in different places, so there are some markets
and niches where people might gather on Facebook. There are some ... maybe they're in
Facebook groups, maybe they're following certain thought leaders on Facebook. Maybe
they more hang out on YouTube. Maybe they hang out on LinkedIn, or Twitter, or
Instagram, or Pinterest. So you've got these various different social media platforms and
new ones are coming out all the time, and some of them attract ... well, they all attract
different demographics, so you have to look and say, "Where are my people hanging out?
Where are the people in my market, the people I want to reach, the people I want to sell
to? Where are they hanging out?" And then you have to go find them. You have to study
them.
You have to look at what blogs they hang out at. Find the blogs that ... I mean, this
is like just search on Google -- just search for the main keywords. If you want to teach
people how to play guitar, just go to Google and type in "learn guitar", "learn guitar
blogs", "learn guitar forums". Go to Amazon and search for books on learning to play
guitar. Look at the top books -- read the reviews. Go to the forums; type in whatever the
key phrase is -- "learn guitar forums", "learn blues guitar forums", "electric guitar
forums". Find the forums where people are hanging out at. Look at the most popular
threads -- read through them. Spend this time ... It's amazing the research that you can do
for free.
Go to the blogs that serve whatever your market you're in and look at what posts
are most popular; look at the posts that have the most comments. Read through all the
comments. Read through all the reviews on Amazon. Go to the Facebook groups and look
at the threads that are most popular and read through them -- and this is amazing
research. This is market research that you absolutely couldn't have done a few years ago,

and you will get to know your people, get to know your market. Spend time where they
hang out -- this is time well spent. It's incredibly well spent. Read all their posts; read all
their comments.
Right now, I'm in the middle of launching a product, and at this moment in time,
right now, there's been over a thousand comments left on my launch pages. I've read
every single one of those comments. You want to read all of them. You want to watch the
language they use, watch the phrases they use -- you want to focus on what they care
about. Find the products they're buying. Study the marketing in the products they're
buying. Find out what products are super popular and then study that marketing. What
does their marketing ... what is the copy? What are those videos? What does that tell you
about your target market? What does it tell you about their objections? What does it tell
you about their hot points, about their aspirations, about what keeps them up at night?
Now here's something that a lot of people skip -- you want to buy those products.
You want to buy the popular products in your market and look how those products are
put together. As you look at how they're put together, you can start to figure out more
about your avatar -- that's the phrase we use for your average, typical customer. You want
to sit down and brainstorm -- what are the top three things that keep them up at night?
What are the top three things keeping your people up? What are their top three objections
to moving forward in their lives, to buying products, to getting help? What are their top
three objections?
What are the top three dangers in their life? And by dangers, I mean dangers to
whatever they want to accomplish, right? So if I'm teaching people that ... how to start a
business, how to grow their business, well what are top three dangers? I know one of the
dangers is that they just get overwhelmed and never move into action, right? That's
certainly a danger. Another danger is they spend too much time doing the things that
aren't important, like worrying about logos and worrying about what camera to use.
Another danger would be that they get into a market that is just a nonstarter, that ... They
come up with this idea that just ... it's a nonstarter. They think that they have to come up
with something new that's never been done before when I know that the best thing is if
they go into a market where there are already people selling successfully in that market.
You have to look at the top three opportunities or aspirations for the people in your
market. So, again, I'm teaching entrepreneurs, so what are the three opportunities ... I
think most of the opportunities are around the freedom that people can have in their life -
- the freedom to work when they want, where they want, with who they want to make the

impact they want. And then what are the top three strengths, or secrets, that they don't
know about yet? What are three strengths or secrets that they don't know about yet?
Again, for me, I could name a bunch of them. I'm not sure if I got the top three at the top
of my head, but if I'm teaching people who want to be entrepreneurs, what I know is that
... just how doable it is if they have persistence. How this ... There's really nothing special
about me other than the fact that I got started and I kept moving forward day after day.
That's certainly a strength.
I know that they have capabilities they haven't found yet because I'm sitting here
talking to you -- I never knew I could record an audio program. I never knew I could
shoot a video or that I could perform in front of a camera, and here I am sitting with
millions and millions of views of my videos. Since you're further ahead, since you are not
your market, since you're further down the path, there are things you know that your
prospects don't know, that the people in your market don't know. You have to focus on
those. So, top three things that keep them up at night, top three objections, top three
dangers, top three opportunities or aspirations, and the top three strengths or secrets that
they don't know about.
Okay, so then what you need to do is you need to start building your presence. By
that, I mean go to where ... all those places I talked about they might be hanging out -- in
the Facebook groups, in the forums, on the blogs -- and you start participating and
adding value wherever they're hanging out. Wherever you can, you start to add
value, and you can do this right away. You can like literally stop this recording and go do
this right now. There is nothing stopping you from going to a forum and leaving an
intelligent comment. Start creating a username and leaving an intelligent comment.
Same thing, go to a blog where there's a discussion happening, where there's comments
on a blog, and leave an intelligent comment on that blog. Go to a Facebook group and be
helpful in that group. You're not trying to sell anything; you're not trying to get them to
your website; you're not doing anything but being helpful at this point.
You have to be mindful of group norms, how groups interact. So you want ... If
you're the new guy coming into a forum, you don't want to come in there and act like
you're the most amazing, wise, and old salt there is. You've got to come in there with
respect and just start building your presence -- start building your presence there. This is
where you learn to talk to your market; this is where you learn to talk to your people.
Next, you want to start building your email list, and this intimidates
people to no end, but remember that no one starts with an email list -- no one does. I

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
didn't start with a list. I didn't start with a list. I started that email newsletter about the
stock market, I started publishing it, and it gradually started growing because I was
putting out value and it grew through word of mouth. Those 80,000 subscribers were
almost entirely built through word of mouth. And then when I started my business, my
current business about entrepreneurship, I started over again from zero. I had that
80,000 subscribers for that investment list, but I wasn't going to send them an email
about, "Hey, go check out my marketing program," because it was not a fit. I started over
again from scratch, from zero. No one starts with a list, but when you have a list,
it's the ultimate power. It's the one true asset.
A lot of people focus on social media and building a social media following ... and
that's great as an add-on, something that's ancillary, but the real power is in your email
list. You use social media as a tool to build your email list -- you don't ... Social is not an
asset. Social is simply something you use to build an email list. So how do you build an
email list? Again, this is a bigger topic than we're gonna cover here in Operation Quick
Start, but it basically comes down to creating a what we call a "lead magnet". This is
something that people would give you their email list ... give you their email address in
exchange for this lead magnet. It could be a video, or a series of videos, or it could be a
PDF, or it could be both a video and a PDF -- that works great. That's what I primarily do.
I usually either do a video or a PDF, but the most successful is a video plus a PDF about
whatever hot topic that they have.
If they want to learn to play guitar, I guarantee you one of the top things is going to
be, "What guitar do I buy?" So if you created a video and a PDF on telling them what
guitar, or how to buy a guitar. You could do another one on how to tune a guitar. Those ...
I mean, that's a good lead magnet. You would get people opting in for that. So go back to
those top three lists -- the three things keeping them up at night, the three objections, the
three dangers, the three opportunities and aspirations, the top three strengths or secrets -
- and create a lead magnet that answers one, or two, or three of those things. Go back to
those forums that you were researching, or look at what are the sticky topics; what are the
most popular topics? What are the things that keep on coming up in either those forums
or the Facebook groups. What keeps on coming up?
Create something that answers one of those burning questions. It doesn't have to
be perfect -- remember done is better than perfect. You can make it better; you can
iterate. Your first one is going to be your worst one. But the thing you want to remember
is in business, it's all about building assets, and when you build a lead magnet, you've
built an asset. I created a lead magnet ... As I record this, it's been 14 years ago and it still
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
gets opt-ins -- not a ton of them, but it still occasionally gets opt-ins after 14 years. A good
lead magnet is a great asset for you and that will be build your business. So, you create
your lead magnet, then you create an opt-in page, or sometimes we call it a "squeeze
page". This is a page where people come to and they can put in their email address and
then they get your lead magnet -- it's that simple.
Now, these days ... and I'll get the tools in the next section, but this used to be really
hard to do, and these days it's really easy to do because there's services that will do this
for you quickly, and easily, and almost automatically. You create your opt-in page, your
squeeze page, then you hook it up to your email list service, and, again, I'll talk about tools
in the next section, but this is the service where when people subscribe, they get added to
a list on your email list service, your "email service provider" we call it, and then you have
this list of people there and then when you want to send an email out to them, you
actually just go to that service. You put in your email, you push the button, and then it
goes out to everyone. When I started, you couldn't even do this. There weren't services
like that. Like I said, I was sending all my emails via blind carbon copy. But things are
better now than they were back then.
So, then, when people sign up, you want to create a sequence for them to get, an
email sequence, what we'd call an "autoresponder sequence". So, you want to get your
first three messages done. The first one is a welcome email and where you're delivering
whatever the lead magnet is. You welcome and then ... So, this is the first email,
"Welcome. Here's your lead magnet. Here's the thing I promised you, and this is what it's
going to do for you." That's what that first email is about. Second email goes out, is like
the next day. The second email is about getting them to consume the lead magnet,
whatever it is -- if it's a video or if it's a PDF -- having them actually open it up and get
into it. This might sound surprising, but a lot of times people put in their email address
and they'll get the PDF, but they won't actually open it. You have to get them to consume
it. It's important to get them to consume it because that's where you start building a
relationship.
That's where you start having a connection with them. That helps to build your
authority -- it introduces you to them. The third email will be a little bit of backstory of
who you are, and why they should care, and what you can do for them. Have some type of
a lesson around whatever your product is and tie it back to the lead magnet and getting
them to consume it. So, those are the first three emails you want to get done. This isn't
gonna take you a long time at all. What we're talking about here is create your lead
magnet, and that might take you some time. It might take you a few hours, or a day, or
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

two days to get it done. Creating that squeeze page, once you get the right service, is not
hard. You can create that squeeze page and hook it up to your email service in probably a
few hours, and then get those three emails written. Let's say that takes you two or three
hours. So, in the course of a couple days here, you have the basics for starting to build an
email list.
Then it's a matter of sending traffic to that squeeze page. At this point, if you've
been building your presence in social media and those Facebook groups, or wherever your
people are hanging out ... If you've been building your presence on the forums, you can go
there and you can say, "Hey, folks, I've got this cool new thing. I've got a video and a PDF
that is all about how to buy your first guitar. Go check it out here.' Again, you have to
watch the group norms because sometimes in a forum they don't want you sending people
to an opt-in page, so you have to watch that. But there are places where you will be able to
post that, either in forums, or in social media, or on your Facebook page, but you start
putting it out that way.
Say there's a forum that you have become a member and you've built some kind of
a presence there, and maybe they don't like you sending people to an opt-in page or a
squeeze page, you can go on there and say ... Say it's a guitar forum, and it is about how to
buy your first guitar, you can go there and say, "Hey, folks, I just created a video and a
PDF on how to buy your first guitar. Here's the PDF," and you just upload it to the forum
and let people have it for free and build even more value there. And then maybe people
are like, "That's great, and you said you had a video, too?" And you're like, "Yeah. Here, go
check out my video," and then you give them the opt-in page. You have to figure out what
the norms are, and by the norms I mean the normal actions -- the way they want you to
participate. But there's almost always a way once you start building value in a given venue
to gradually pull people over to your opt-in pages and start getting people on your list.
Another way you could do this is with paid traffic. These day, Facebook traffic
works for many, many, many markets -- many markets. You don't want to just go start
spending money willy-nilly, but ultimately this is a great way to build your list -- is with
paid traffic going from an ad into your opt-in page.

immediately -- even if you don't have an offer. Even if you don't have something to sell,
you start building your list. I did this in every market I've ever been in, and the two

primary markets was the stock market niche and my current niche which is
entrepreneurship. But both times, I immediately ... Soon as I decided I wanted to get in
that business, I started building a list.
I put together a lead magnet. I put together an opt-in page and I did my best to
drive traffic to that page even before ... long before I had anything to sell. But you should
always be thinking about what your offer could be -- start thinking about that right away.
You want to keep on getting to know your people. All that research you initially did on
your market, but now that you started to build a list you want to look at things like what
type of subject lines did they open? Like, when you send your emails, some of your emails
will be more popular than others. Sometimes people will get them and ... Well, some
people don't know this, that when you're publishing via email, most of the people on your
email list actually won't even open your email. That's just a fact of life.
But you'll be able to see which ones they do open. You actually will get stats on this
from your email service provider, so maybe one email 25% of the people opened, and
another one 37% opened, and another one 5% opened -- that will vary based on your
subject line. So watch what subject lines resonate with them. What emails do they react
to? What emails do they click on? What emails do they respond to, do they reply to?
When you put up videos or blog posts, what ones do they react to? How do they react?
What ... I mean, what we're getting at is what do they care about? What gets their
attention? The research you did in other forums and other venues is great, now we're
taking it to the next step. Now that you're starting to gather your own tribe, now you're
tapping into what they react to.
Consider doing a Facebook Live. You can go on Facebook, you can start a live
broadcast, and you can get in front of people. There's other places you can get in front of
them live. You can do it with Instagram; you can do it with SnapChat; you can do it with
YouTube -- and it's growing. I mean, live is growing. Live video is growing. So, when you
get in front of them, and start interacting with them, and answering their questions, now
you're really getting a feel for the market all in service of what are you going to actually
offer? Answer questions that come in via email. Get in conversations with them via email.
Always look for ways to interact and have conversations. Ask them about what they're
excited about, what they're worried about, what they want to accomplish in the next three
months. What's holding them back? Their biggest fears. This is getting to know your
people. This is going to help you figure out what you should offer them.
Once you're getting close to what you should offer them, your next step is a

Seed Launch. This is the easiest way to create your first product. It's beyond the scope
of this course, but if you have access to my Product Launch Formula program, it's covered
in depth in Product Launch Formula. I go even deeper in my Seed Launch Deep Dive.
This is the easiest way to create a program. It's the easiest way to create a product -- and
you actually get paid for the product before you create it. It's amazing that way. The thing
is... it's basically - you create it in conjunction with your audience, and it's super
interactive. What it means is you end up with a great product. It's a great product.
Right now, I just want to have one quick aside. So I have a lot of people get involved
in my world and they're like, "I'm going to write a book, or I wrote a book, or I'm almost
finished with a book," and the thing you need to understand is a book is not a business. I
mean, you might have worked hard in your book and congratulations -- that's awesome --
but the world doesn't care that you wrote a book. The reality is almost no one makes
money from a book. I mean, if you don't want money and if you just want to get your
message out there, then that's fine. That's cool -- a book will work great for that ... but
actually, it won't work because the reality is no one's going to know it because there's tens
of thousands of books that come out every year. So to get people to actually buy it, and
open it, and read it -- it's hard to do unless you have a serious presence.
It's hard to build a serious presence unless you have some type of a product
because a product is what ... an offer is what sustains your business. It's what allows you
to spend the time to build your tribe, to build your list. It's what allows you to advertise,
to drive people to your opt-in page. That's all dependent on an offer, and a book ... What
does a book sell for? $10, $15, maybe tops $29? You're not going to sell enough of them to
create a sustainable business. So unless you already have a great, big presence and a
great, big list ... even if you just don't care to make money, you just want to change the
world by getting people to read your book ... Unless you already have a great, big tribe,
you're not going to be able to do that. If you don't already have a tribe, then no one's ever
gonna hear about your book. So you have to take that book and you have to create a
follow-on course or product. That's the only way this works if your book is a fiction.
If it's a novel, well that's a whole different discussion, but one book's not gonna cut
it. You're gonna need multiple books, and you need to build a following to sell multiple
books to. Don't think that just because you wrote a book the world's gonna beat a path to
your door -- they're not. It comes down to creating a higher priced offer so that you can
take the time, and the effort, and the money to drive people into your business, join your
list, and then you can sell them products, and you can sell them books, and then you can
change the world. Then you can make a real income.

The next thing ... after that first Seed Launch, you keep on building your list, and
then you build out the full product based on what you learned in your seed launch. Once
you have a full out product and a full out offer, then the math will start to work for you.
What I mean by that is you can afford to advertise to bring people into your lead magnet,
into your opt-in page, and get them to opt-in and join your list, and then you can sell
them the product.
So, just to reiterate here, first thing you do -- you figure out what your
market is. After you figure out what your market is, you do some research
by hanging out where they hang out -- in the forums, in social media, on
blogs. Check out the Amazon reviews for books in your market. Get to know your people
really, really well. Figure out where their pain points are -- their objections, their dangers,
their opportunities -- and then start building your presence in those ... Wherever they're
hanging out, start building your presence.
Then you need to start to build your list. You create a lead magnet;
you create an opt-in, and you start driving traffic there. Then you do a Seed
Launch, and then eventually you take what you've learned in that seed
launch and you build it out into a full offer.
So, I just compressed the entire start of your business, but that's the path you need.
